items.fix: Evaluate only on held-out validation or test labels the model did not train on.
items.why: Training scores are optimistically ranked — AUC inflates and model selection becomes unreliable.
items.example: Wrong: roc_auc_score(y_train, clf.predict_proba(X_train)[:, 1]). Right: same call on X_test, y_test.

items.mistake: Passing binary predictions instead of continuous scores
items.fix: Use predict_proba[:, 1], decision_function, or raw logits — not predict() hard labels.
items.why: Hard 0/1 outputs collapse the threshold sweep to one or two points; AUC loses ranking meaning.
items.example: Wrong: roc_auc_score(y_test, clf.predict(X_test)). Right: roc_auc_score(y_test, clf.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]).

items.mistake: Treating high AUC as proof of good calibration
items.fix: Report calibration plots or Brier score separately when probabilistic ŷ drive decisions or pricing.
items.why: AUC is invariant to monotonic score warping — a model can rank well while assigning ŷ = 0.51 to almost everyone.
items.example: AUC 0.92 but all scores in [0.48, 0.52] — great ranking possible with useless absolute probabilities.

items.mistake: Using ROC-AUC alone on extreme imbalance without checking precision
items.fix: Also plot precision-recall curve or PR-AUC and inspect confusion matrix at a realistic threshold.
items.why: Low FPR can coexist with unacceptable precision when negatives are abundant — ROC looks deceptively strong.
items.example: Fraud 0.5% base rate: AUC 0.95 but precision 0.08 at threshold giving 90% recall — check PR curve.
